section.title: The Song of Deborah
section.sectionRationale: The prose victory of chapter 4 now bursts into song. Deborah and Barak praise the LORD who marched from Seir, remember the days of oppression when the highways were empty, and celebrate the tribes who volunteered while naming those who stayed home. The hymn crowns Jael as most blessed among women and lingers, with terrible pathos, over Sisera's mother waiting at the window for a son who will never return. It ends with a prayer that God's enemies perish and His friends shine like the sun.
section.thesis: The deliverance belongs to the LORD, so His people answer with praise: they bless Him for going before them, honor those who risked their lives, mourn the failure of those who held back, exalt the God who saves through the lowly, and pray that all His enemies perish while those who love Him blaze like the rising sun.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the call to bless the LORD, verses 1 to 11; the tribes who came and those who stayed, verses 12 to 18; the battle at the Kishon, verses 19 to 23; Jael's deed, verses 24 to 27; and Sisera's mother at the window, verses 28 to 31.

units.label: Bless the LORD who marched from Seir
units.note: praise for the God who goes before His people

verses.text: On that day Deborah and {{barak|Barak}} son of Abinoam sang this song:
“When the princes take the lead in Israel, when the people volunteer, bless the LORD.
Listen, O kings! Give ear, O princes! I will sing to the LORD; I will sing praise to the LORD, the God of Israel.
O LORD, when You went out from Seir, when You marched from the land of Edom, the earth trembled, the heavens poured out rain, and the clouds poured down water.
The mountains quaked before the LORD, the One of Sinai, before the LORD, the God of Israel.
In the days of Shamgar son of Anath, in the days of Jael, the highways were deserted and the travelers took the byways.
Life in the villages ceased; it ended in Israel, until I, {{deborah|Deborah}}, {{qum|arose}}, a {{em|mother}} in Israel.
When they chose new gods, then war came to their gates. Not a shield or spear was found among forty thousand in Israel.
My heart is with the princes of Israel, with the volunteers among the people. Bless the LORD!
You who ride white donkeys, who sit on saddle blankets, and you who travel the road, ponder

the voices of the singers at the watering places. There they shall recount the righteous acts of the LORD, the righteous deeds of His villagers in Israel. Then the people of the LORD went down to the gates:

units.label: The tribes who came and those who stayed

verses.text: ‘Awake, awake, O Deborah! Awake, awake, sing a song! Arise, O Barak, and take hold of your captives, O son of Abinoam!’
Then the survivors came down to the nobles; the people of the LORD came down to me against the mighty.
Some came from Ephraim, with their roots in Amalek; Benjamin came with your people after you. The commanders came down from Machir, the bearers of the marshal’s staff from Zebulun.
The princes of Issachar were with Deborah, and Issachar was with Barak, rushing into the valley at his heels. In the clans of Reuben there was great indecision.
Why did you sit among the sheepfolds to hear the whistling for the flocks? In the clans of Reuben there was great indecision.
Gilead remained beyond the Jordan. Dan, why did you linger by the ships? Asher stayed at the coast and remained in his harbors.
Zebulun was a people who risked their lives; Naphtali, too, on the heights of the battlefield.
Kings came and fought; then the kings of Canaan fought at Taanach by the waters of Megiddo, but they took no plunder of silver.
From the heavens the stars fought; from their courses they fought against Sisera.
The River Kishon swept them away, the ancient river, the River Kishon. March on, O my soul, in strength!
Then the hooves of horses thundered, the mad galloping of his stallions.
‘Curse Meroz,’ says the angel of the LORD. ‘Bitterly curse her inhabitants; for they did not come to help the LORD, to help the LORD against the mighty.’
Most blessed among women is Jael, the wife of Heber the Kenite, most blessed of tent-dwelling women.
He asked for water, and she gave him milk. In a magnificent bowl she brought him curds.
She reached for the tent peg, her right hand for the workman’s hammer. She struck Sisera and crushed his skull; she shattered and pierced his temple.
At her feet he collapsed, he fell, there he lay still; at her feet he collapsed, he fell; where he collapsed, there he fell dead.

units.label: Sisera's mother at the window
units.note: so may all Your enemies perish, O LORD

verses.text: Sisera’s mother looked through the window; she peered through the lattice and lamented: ‘Why is his chariot so long in coming? What has delayed the clatter of his chariots?’
Her wisest ladies answer; indeed she keeps telling herself,
‘Are they not finding and dividing the spoil, a girl or two for each warrior, a plunder of dyed garments for Sisera, the spoil of embroidered garments for the neck of the looter?’
So may all Your enemies perish, O LORD! But may those who love You shine like the sun at its brightest.” And the land had rest for forty years.

barak.gloss: The name Barak means lightning. It is fitting for a song in which the LORD marches with storm and rain, the stars fight from heaven, and the Kishon sweeps the chariots away. The lightning-named commander is only an instrument; the true power flashing over the battlefield is the LORD of Sinai.

deborah.gloss: The name Deborah means bee. The small, unlikely bee becomes a mother in Israel who rouses a nation and sings its victory hymn, a picture of God saving through the lowly and overlooked rather than the mighty.

qum.gloss: To arise, stand up, take action. Deborah says she arose as a mother in Israel when life in the villages had ceased, and the song calls Barak to arise and take his captives. To arise is to act in faith on what the LORD is doing, rising from paralysis to God-given deliverance.

em.gloss: Mother. Deborah calls herself a mother in Israel, one who protects, gathers, and stirs her people to courage. The word returns with bitter irony at the song's end in Sisera's mother, waiting at the window for a son who lies dead, two mothers set against each other by the LORD's deliverance.

basic.context: Judges 5 is the victory hymn that Deborah and Barak sing after the LORD defeats Sisera. It opens by blessing the LORD, remembering how He marched from Seir with earth-shaking power, and recalling the misery of the oppression, when roads were unsafe and villages emptied, until Deborah arose as a mother in Israel. The song honors the tribes who volunteered for battle and openly shames those who stayed by their flocks and ships. It retells the battle in vivid poetry, the stars fighting from heaven and the ancient Kishon sweeping the chariots away, then crowns Jael as most blessed among women for felling Sisera. Finally it turns, with piercing pathos, to Sisera's mother at her window, imagining spoil while her son lies dead, and closes with a prayer that all God's enemies perish while those who love Him shine like the rising sun.
basic.hermeneutics: This is inspired Hebrew poetry celebrating a real deliverance, so it uses vivid imagery, storm, stars, and a flooding river, to declare that the LORD Himself won the battle. It describes and praises what God did; it is not a step-by-step prescription. The blessing of Jael and the taunt over Sisera's mother belong to a war-hymn celebrating God's rescue of an oppressed people, honest about the cost of the enemy's defeat, not a rulebook for personal revenge. The song's abiding call is to bless the LORD and to give ourselves wholly to His cause.
basic.originalLanguages: The two named leaders carry meaning: Barak means lightning, and the song is full of storm and heavenly warfare; Deborah means bee, the small and unlikely one whom God uses. She says she arose, qum, as a mother, em, in Israel, and the song ends with another mother, Sisera's, at the window, the two set against each other by God's deliverance.
basic.typology: The LORD marching from Seir amid storm and quaking mountains, fighting from the heavens for His people, foreshadows Christ, the divine warrior who comes to defeat His people's enemies. The prayer that God's enemies perish and His lovers shine like the sun anticipates the final judgment and the glory of the redeemed, who will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father.
basic.christ: The whole song insists that the LORD fought and won; the stars, the storm, and the river did what human chariots could not. This points to Christ, the true Warrior-King, who fights for His helpless people and triumphs over every enemy. The closing prayer, that those who love the LORD shine like the sun at its brightest, is fulfilled in Christ, who says the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father, and in Him who is the Sun of Righteousness rising with healing.
basic.god: God is the divine warrior who comes with earth-shaking power to rescue His people, and the One who alone deserves the praise. He works through the willing and the lowly, honors those who risk themselves for His cause, and takes offense when His people hold back. He is just: His enemies perish, and those who love Him are made to shine. The song teaches His people to answer His salvation with wholehearted worship.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 15 is the earlier song of the sea, celebrating the LORD as a warrior. Psalm 68 echoes God marching out and the earth trembling. Matthew 13 promises that the righteous will shine like the sun in the Father's kingdom. And Revelation 19 shows Christ the divine warrior defeating the enemies of His people.
basic.oneStory: The Song of Deborah teaches God's people how to remember and celebrate His salvation: with praise that gives Him all the glory, honesty about who joined the cause and who did not, and hope that His enemies will fall while His friends shine. It looks back to the song of the sea and forward to the final victory of Christ, the true Warrior-King, in whom those who love God will shine like the sun forever.

context.literary: Judges 5 is one of the oldest and most vivid poems in the Bible, a victory hymn set beside the prose account of chapter 4. It moves in swift, image-rich strophes: a call to praise, a recital of the LORD's coming in storm, a muster-roll of the tribes, the battle told with cosmic imagery, the blessing of Jael, and the haunting scene of Sisera's mother. Its poetry heightens what the prose reports, so that the reader feels the earth quake and the river run.
context.historical: The song presupposes the same events as chapter 4: Jabin's oppression, the muster at Tabor, and the rout at the Kishon in the Jezreel Valley near Taanach and Megiddo. Its picture of empty highways and disarmed villagers fits a time of Canaanite dominance, and the swollen, sweeping Kishon points to a sudden storm miring the iron chariots. The tribes praised and rebuked, Ephraim, Benjamin, Machir, Zebulun, Issachar, Naphtali against Reuben, Gilead, Dan, and Asher, reflect the loose, often fractured unity of Israel before the monarchy.
context.canonical: The song stands in the tradition of Israel's victory poetry, especially the song of the sea in Exodus 15 and the war psalms such as Psalm 68 and Psalm 83. Its portrait of the LORD as divine warrior who fights from heaven runs on to the New Testament vision of Christ the warrior in Revelation 19, and its closing image of the righteous shining like the sun is taken up by Jesus in Matthew 13:43. Within Judges it interprets the deliverance theologically, insisting that the glory is God's alone.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To praise the LORD as the true deliverer of Israel, to teach the people to remember His salvation rightly, to honor those who gave themselves to His cause and shame those who held back, and to fix hope on a God whose enemies perish while His lovers shine.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The song describes and celebrates a specific deliverance in poetic form; it does not prescribe holy war, or the blessing of Jael's method, or the taunting of an enemy's mother as patterns for personal conduct. What endures is its call to bless the LORD for His salvation, to commit fully to His cause rather than sit on the sidelines, and to trust that God will judge His enemies and glorify those who love Him.

debates.question: Why does the song bless Jael so highly for a deed of deception and violence?

views.label: She is praised as God's instrument of deliverance
views.case: Within a hymn celebrating the LORD's rescue of an oppressed people, Jael's decisive act ends the tyrant who had crushed Israel; she is called most blessed among women, verse 24, as the one through whom God fulfilled His word.

views.label: The praise is for the outcome, not a blanket endorsement of her methods
views.case: The song celebrates the deliverance God accomplished through her, in the setting of open war against a brutal oppressor, without turning her deception into a general moral rule for private life.

debates.bearing: The blessing honors God's saving act through an unlikely hand and magnifies His power to deliver, while the passage does not present Jael's deception as a pattern for personal ethics; it leaves the reader marvelling at grace, not licensed to imitate treachery.

debates.question: What is the point of the closing scene of Sisera's mother at the window?

views.label: A window into the horror of the oppressor's world
views.case: Her ladies imagine the army sharing out captured girls and dyed garments as spoil, exposing the casual cruelty of Sisera's regime and helping the reader feel that his fall is just judgment, not mere tragedy.

views.label: A note of real human pathos amid victory
views.case: The song lingers on a grieving mother watching for a son who will never come home, a sober reminder that even a just deliverance carries the weight of real death and loss.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold together: the scene both vindicates God's judgment on a cruel oppressor and refuses to make war painless, so the closing prayer for God's enemies to perish is weighty and serious, not glib.

study.originalLanguages: The song's meaning is carried partly by its names. Barak means lightning, and the hymn crackles with it: the LORD marches from Seir with rain and quaking mountains, the stars fight from their courses, and the ancient Kishon sweeps the chariots away, so the man named lightning is only a spark under the true storm of God. Deborah means bee, the small and unlikely creature; yet this bee arose, qum, as a mother, em, in Israel when the villages had fallen silent. To arise is to act in faith on what the LORD is doing, and Deborah rouses a paralyzed people to rise with her. The word em, mother, frames the whole poem with terrible irony: Deborah the protecting mother in Israel at the start, and at the close Sisera's mother at the window, waiting for a son who lies dead at Jael's feet. Two mothers, set against each other by the LORD's deliverance, and between them the repeated command, bless the LORD, so that every image finally returns the glory to God.

typology.type: The LORD marching from Seir amid storm and quaking mountains
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the divine warrior who comes with power to defeat His people's enemies, Habakkuk 3:3-13 and Revelation 19:11-16.

typology.type: The stars and the river fighting for Israel
typology.fulfillment: God bending all creation to save His people, fulfilled in Christ who commands wind and wave and disarms the powers, Colossians 2:15.

typology.type: Those who love the LORD shining like the sun
typology.fulfillment: The righteous shining like the sun in the kingdom of their Father, Matthew 13:43, in Christ the Sun of Righteousness, Malachi 4:2.

study.christ: The whole hymn insists that the LORD fought and won: the storm, the stars, and the swollen Kishon accomplished what Israel's disarmed villagers never could. This points to Christ, the true Warrior-King, who fights for a helpless people and triumphs over every enemy, and who wins His greatest victory not with chariots but through the weakness of the cross and the power of resurrection. The song's closing prayer, that those who love the LORD shine like the sun at its brightest, is taken up by Jesus Himself, who promises that the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father, Matthew 13:43. He is the Sun of Righteousness who rises with healing in His wings, Malachi 4:2, and Revelation shows Him coming as the divine warrior to judge His enemies and vindicate His own, Revelation 19:11-16. Deborah's song, sung after one deliverance, teaches the church to sing to the Warrior who has won the final deliverance in Christ.
study.god: The song is worship, and its subject is the LORD. He is the divine warrior who comes from Seir with rain, earthquake, and heavenly armies, before whom the mountains quake; He is the God of Sinai who covenanted with Israel and now fights for them. He works through the willing and the lowly, a bee named Deborah, a tent-dwelling woman named Jael, and He honors those who risk their lives for His cause while rebuking, even cursing through His angel, those who would not come to His help. He is just: His enemies perish, and those who love Him are made to shine like the sun. Above all He is the One to whom His rescued people must return their praise, bless the LORD, again and again, so that the deliverance ends not in self-congratulation but in worship.

crossReferences.note: The song of the sea, Israel's earlier victory hymn celebrating the LORD as a warrior who drowned Pharaoh's chariots, the pattern behind Deborah's song.
God going out before His people so that the earth quakes and the heavens pour down, echoing the LORD's march from Seir in verse 4.
Jesus promises that the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father, fulfilling the song's closing hope in verse 31.
Christ the divine warrior rides out to judge and make war in righteousness, the ultimate fulfillment of the LORD who fights for His people.

study.oneStory: The Song of Deborah teaches God's people how to remember His salvation: with praise that gives Him all the glory, honesty about who joined His cause and who held back, sober reckoning with the cost of the enemy's fall, and hope that His foes will perish while His friends shine like the sun. It looks back to the song of the sea and forward to the final victory of Christ, the true Warrior-King, in whom those who love God will shine like the sun in their Father's kingdom forever. The land had rest for forty years, but the song reaches for a rest and a King that only Christ will bring.

quiz.q: What is the main purpose of the Song of Deborah?

options.text: To bless and praise the LORD for the deliverance He won for His people.
options.note: The refrain bless the LORD and verse 3 show the song's aim is worship of God the deliverer.

options.text: To celebrate Barak's military genius.
options.note: Barak is honored, but the song credits the victory to the LORD, not to Barak's skill.

options.text: To record a neutral, factual battle report.
options.note: It is a poetic hymn of praise, not a dry report; its purpose is worship.

options.text: To boast in Israel's strength and weapons.
options.note: Israel had no shield or spear among forty thousand; the song glories in God, not in arms.

quiz.q: How does the song portray the LORD's role in the battle?

options.text: As the divine warrior who marched in storm, whose stars and river fought Sisera.
options.note: Verses 4-5 and 20-21 depict the LORD Himself fighting from heaven and through the Kishon.

options.text: As a distant observer who let the tribes fight alone.
options.note: The tribes fought, but the song insists the LORD Himself marched and fought; He was not distant.

options.text: As one who only helped after Israel had already won.
options.note: The LORD's action causes the victory; He does not merely arrive afterward.

options.text: As uninvolved in the outcome.
options.note: The whole song credits the deliverance to the LORD; He is anything but uninvolved.

quiz.q: Why does the song both praise some tribes and rebuke others?

options.text: To honor those who risked their lives for the LORD's cause and shame those who held back.
options.note: Verses 14-18 praise Zebulun and Naphtali and rebuke Reuben, Dan, and Asher for staying away.

options.text: To settle old political scores between the tribes.
options.note: There is real rebuke, but the concern is faithfulness to the LORD's cause, not mere politics.

options.text: To show that it did not matter who came to fight.
options.note: The song makes clear it did matter; volunteering for God's cause is honored, holding back is shamed.

options.text: To praise every tribe equally regardless of action.
options.note: The song deliberately distinguishes the willing from those who stayed by their flocks and ships.

quiz.q: How does the song treat Jael's killing of Sisera?

options.text: As a blessed act of deliverance, calling her most blessed among women.
options.note: Verse 24 crowns Jael as most blessed for felling the oppressor within God's rescue.

options.text: As a simple rule that God's people may deceive and kill their enemies.
options.note: Her deed is celebrated as deliverance, but the song does not turn her method into a general rule.

options.text: As a shameful act the song condemns.
options.note: The song praises, not condemns, the deliverance God brought through her.

options.text: As an event with no significance in the victory.
options.note: Her act is the climax of the deliverance and is highly honored in the song.

quiz.q: What is the hope expressed in the song's closing prayer?

options.text: That all God's enemies perish while those who love Him shine like the sun.
options.note: Verse 31 prays this and points forward to Matthew 13:43 and the glory of the redeemed.

options.text: That Israel would never have to fight again.
options.note: The land had rest, but the closing prayer is about God's enemies perishing and His lovers shining, not the end of all conflict.

options.text: That the tribes would gain great wealth and spoil.
options.note: Spoil appears only in the mouth of Sisera's mother; the prayer is for God's justice and His people's glory.

options.text: That Sisera's family would be comforted.
options.note: The prayer is that God's enemies perish; the mother's scene underlines the justice of Sisera's fall.

questions: The song ends where it begins, bless the LORD. How readily do you turn your own rescues and mercies into worship that gives God the glory, rather than quiet self-congratulation?
The tribes are remembered either for coming to help the LORD or for staying by their flocks and ships. Where is God calling you off the sidelines and into His cause right now?
Deborah arose as a mother in Israel when others were paralyzed. Who around you needs someone to rise in faith and rouse them to courage, and could that person be you?
The song insists the stars and the river fought for Israel, so the glory is God's. How does remembering that God fights for you steady you when the odds look impossible?
The prayer is that those who love the LORD shine like the sun at its brightest, fulfilled in Christ. How does the hope of shining in the Father's kingdom shape how you live and endure today?

translationNotes.note: The line rendered when they chose new gods, then war came to their gates is difficult in Hebrew; some render it they chose new gods, then there was war in the gates, and the picture of not a shield or spear among forty thousand stresses Israel's utter helplessness before God delivered them.

The address to those who ride white donkeys, sit on saddle blankets, and travel the road gathers rich and poor, mounted and walking; the exact terms are uncertain, but the sense is a call for all classes to ponder and recount the LORD's righteous acts.

The BSB footnotes flag uncertainty here: the word for singers can also mean archers or those who divide the flocks, and His villagers can be read as His warriors; the thrust is that God's saving deeds are recounted where the people gather.

The phrase with their roots in Amalek is obscure; it may mean Ephraim whose territory once bordered Amalek, and the marshal's staff from Zebulun points to officers who mustered the fighters, part of the muster-roll of tribes.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 5:1-31, the Song of Deborah, rendered verse by verse as poetry. Poem and lineBreak markers ignored; string segments per verse concatenated in order. Two em dashes in the BSB source, at verses 22 and 30, were converted to commas; no em or en dashes anywhere in the file. Footnote markers omitted. Sermon set to none; no trusted-cluster sermon on Judges 5 verified this session.
